panels.description: Total on-disk size of the current database (heap + indexes + toast + WAL is not counted).
panels.title: Database total

panels.description: Per-schema rollup of total size and object counts. User schemas only.

targets.rawSql: SELECT n.nspname AS schema, pg_size_pretty(SUM(pg_total_relation_size(c.oid))) AS total, COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE c.relkind='r') AS tables, COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE c.relkind='i') AS indexes FROM pg_class c JOIN pg_namespace n ON n.oid = c.relnamespace WHERE n.nspname NOT IN ('pg_catalog','information_schema') GROUP BY n.nspname ORDER BY SUM(pg_total_relation_size(c.oid)) DESC

panels.title: Per-schema rollup

panels.description: Top 20 tables by total size (heap + indexes + toast). est_rows is from planner statistics (pg_class.reltuples) and is refreshed by autovacuum/ANALYZE.

targets.rawSql: SELECT n.nspname || '.' || c.relname AS "table", pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(c.oid)) AS total, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(c.oid)) AS heap, pg_size_pretty(pg_indexes_size(c.oid)) AS indexes, pg_size_pretty(COALESCE(pg_total_relation_size(c.reltoastrelid),0)) AS toast, to_char(c.reltuples, 'FM999,999,999,999') AS est_rows FROM pg_class c JOIN pg_namespace n ON n.oid = c.relnamespace WHERE c.relkind IN ('r','m') AND n.nspname NOT IN ('pg_catalog','information_schema') ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(c.oid) DESC LIMIT 20

panels.title: Top 20 tables by total size

panels.description: Top 30 indexes by on-disk size. Useful for spotting bloated or over-broad indexes.

targets.rawSql: SELECT schemaname || '.' || tablename AS "table", indexname, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(format('%I.%I', schemaname, indexname)::regclass)) AS size, LEFT(indexdef, 120) AS definition FROM pg_indexes WHERE schemaname NOT IN ('pg_catalog','information_schema') ORDER BY pg_relation_size(format('%I.%I', schemaname, indexname)::regclass) DESC LIMIT 30

panels.title: Top 30 indexes by size

panels.description: When the postgres stat counters were last reset (manual reset, restart, or crash). idx_scan = 0 for indexes created after this time means 'truly unused'; before this time it could mean 'we forgot.' As a rule of thumb, don't trust the Unused panel until at least a week after this timestamp.

targets.rawSql: SELECT EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM stats_reset) * 1000 AS reset_at FROM pg_stat_database WHERE datname = current_database()

panels.title: Stats counters last reset

panels.description: Indexes that have never been scanned. Unique and primary-key indexes are excluded — they enforce constraints even when they have no scan activity. Read with the 'Stats counters last reset' panel above: an idx_scan of 0 only means 'unused' if enough time has passed since the reset.

targets.rawSql: SELECT s.schemaname || '.' || s.relname AS "table", s.indexrelname AS index, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(s.indexrelid)) AS size FROM pg_stat_user_indexes s JOIN pg_index i ON i.indexrelid = s.indexrelid WHERE s.idx_scan = 0 AND NOT i.indisunique AND NOT i.indisprimary ORDER BY pg_relation_size(s.indexrelid) DESC

panels.title: Unused indexes (idx_scan = 0)

panels.description: Indexes that get scanned but are large relative to their usage. A big index with a small scans count is a candidate for review — either it's overly broad (consider partial / smaller composite), or it serves a rare query that doesn't justify the size.

targets.rawSql: SELECT s.schemaname || '.' || s.relname AS "table", s.indexrelname AS index, s.idx_scan AS scans, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(s.indexrelid)) AS size, s.idx_tup_read, s.idx_tup_fetch FROM pg_stat_user_indexes s JOIN pg_index i ON i.indexrelid = s.indexrelid WHERE s.idx_scan > 0 AND NOT i.indisunique AND NOT i.indisprimary ORDER BY pg_relation_size(s.indexrelid) DESC LIMIT 30

panels.title: Low-scan indexes by size

panels.description: Per-table row count, heap size, and average bytes per row. Avg bytes per row separates 'big because of row count' from 'big because of wide rows / TOAST'. est_rows is the planner estimate (pg_class.reltuples).

targets.rawSql: SELECT n.nspname || '.' || c.relname AS "table", to_char(c.reltuples, 'FM999,999,999,999') AS est_rows, pg_size_pretty(pg_relation_size(c.oid)) AS heap, pg_size_pretty((pg_relation_size(c.oid) / NULLIF(c.reltuples, 0))::bigint) AS avg_bytes_per_row FROM pg_class c JOIN pg_namespace n ON n.oid = c.relnamespace WHERE c.relkind = 'r' AND n.nspname NOT IN ('pg_catalog','information_schema') ORDER BY pg_relation_size(c.oid) DESC LIMIT 30

panels.title: Rows / bytes-per-row / size (all tables)

panels.description: For each major time-stamped table: how long have we been collecting (age_days), how big has the table become, and the implied average growth rate. Assumes roughly linear growth from oldest record to now — a useful sanity check, not a forecast. To track actual growth over time, see docs/todo.md → 'Add postgres_exporter for time-series DB observability'.

targets.rawSql: WITH per_table(label, tname, oldest) AS (VALUES ('extrinsics','extrinsics',(SELECT MIN(created_at) FROM extrinsics)), ('metagraph_block','metagraph_block',(SELECT MIN(timestamp) FROM metagraph_block)), ('metagraph_dump','metagraph_dump',(SELECT MIN(finished_at) FROM metagraph_dump WHERE finished_at IS NOT NULL)), ('subnet_hyperparam_history','subnet_hyperparam_history',(SELECT MIN(created_at) FROM subnet_hyperparam_history))) SELECT pt.label AS "table", EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM pt.oldest) * 1000 AS oldest, (EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now() - pt.oldest)) / 86400)::int AS age_days, pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(format('public.%I', pt.tname)::regclass)) AS total_size, round(pg_total_relation_size(format('public.%I', pt.tname)::regclass)::numeric / NULLIF(EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now() - pt.oldest))/86400, 0) / 1024 / 1024, 2) AS mb_per_day, round(pg_total_relation_size(format('public.%I', pt.tname)::regclass)::numeric / NULLIF(EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (now() - pt.oldest))/86400, 0) / 1024 / 1024 * 365, 0) AS mb_per_year_projected FROM per_table pt WHERE pt.oldest IS NOT NULL ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(format('public.%I', pt.tname)::regclass) DESC

panels.title: Retention focus (per major table)
